_meta.description: Workshop facilitation, user story writing, requirement elicitation techniques, and documentation standards.

mcq.q: What is the main purpose of requirements capture in a PL-600 solution architect engagement?
options: To collect as many feature ideas as possible without validation
To accurately understand customer needs, refine them, and express them in a form that can guide solution decisions
To replace fit/gap analysis entirely
To delay solution direction until development starts
mcq.explanation: Requirements capture is about understanding and refining customer needs so the solution can be evaluated and designed against real business outcomes, not just raw requests.
wrongExplanations: Collecting unvalidated ideas creates noise and scope risk.
Fit/gap analysis still matters after requirements are captured.
Requirements capture should inform solution direction early, not delay it unnecessarily.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: Which workshop behavior best supports effective requirement elicitation?
options: Let the loudest stakeholder define the requirement
Use structured questions, validate assumptions, and separate business needs from solution ideas
Focus only on technical implementation choices
Avoid documenting disagreements to keep momentum
mcq.explanation: Good workshop facilitation uses structured questioning, validation, and clarification to uncover real requirements rather than simply recording opinions or implementation suggestions.
wrongExplanations: This creates bias and weakens requirement quality.
Technical implementation choices are premature during elicitation.
Disagreements should be surfaced and managed, not hidden.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: A stakeholder says, "We need a custom approval app." What should the architect do first?
options: Write a user story for the custom app immediately
Ask what business problem the approval process must solve and what outcome defines success
Estimate the number of approval screens
Assign a developer to prototype the app
mcq.explanation: The architect should first uncover the underlying business need and success outcome before accepting a proposed solution idea.
wrongExplanations: That assumes the proposed implementation is already correct.
Screen estimation is premature.
A prototype before problem clarification can anchor the team on the wrong solution.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

mcq.q: Which user story is written best for solution design and validation?
options: Build an approval page with a comments box
As a service manager, I want high-risk cases routed for approval so that escalations are controlled consistently
Managers need dashboards
Create three custom tables for approvals
mcq.explanation: A strong user story identifies the actor, need, and business value, which helps drive solution and validation decisions.
wrongExplanations: This is an implementation instruction, not a user-centered requirement.
This is too vague and lacks business value context.
This is a design choice rather than a user story.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the strongest reason to document requirements against business processes?
options: It makes the document longer and more detailed
It helps reveal process gaps, dependencies, and where requirements actually apply in the end-to-end flow
It removes the need for stakeholder workshops
It guarantees no customization will be needed
mcq.explanation: Linking requirements to processes gives them operational context and helps the team understand where they matter in the end-to-end scenario.
wrongExplanations: Length is not the goal.
Workshops are still needed to discover and refine the requirements.
Process mapping does not guarantee a no-customization outcome.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

mcq.q: Which question best helps refine a vague requirement during a workshop?
options: Which color should the app use?
What exactly must the user be able to do, under what conditions, and how will you know it works well enough?
How many developers will be assigned?
Should we use plugins or flows?
mcq.explanation: This question forces clarity around behavior, context, and validation, which are key to refining vague statements into usable requirements.
wrongExplanations: Color choice does not refine core requirement meaning.
Staffing does not define the requirement.
Implementation pattern choices are premature.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the best reason to document assumptions alongside captured requirements?
options: To make the backlog look fuller
To expose uncertain conditions that could affect requirement validity, scope, or feasibility
To avoid speaking with stakeholders again
To replace risk management
mcq.explanation: Assumptions identify things believed to be true that may later affect design, scope, and delivery if they prove false.
wrongExplanations: Document size is irrelevant.
Assumptions do not remove the need for more stakeholder input.
Assumptions support risk management but do not replace it.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

mcq.q: Which documentation standard is most useful when capturing architect-level requirements?
options: Record requests exactly as spoken and avoid interpretation
Document requirements clearly, consistently, and with enough context to support design, validation, and scope decisions
Focus only on screenshots of the current system
Store requirements only in email threads
mcq.explanation: Requirements documentation should be clear, structured, and useful for design, testing, and governance rather than being a raw transcript of stakeholder comments.
wrongExplanations: Raw transcription often preserves ambiguity instead of resolving it.
Screenshots can support analysis but do not replace structured requirements.
Email threads are poor as the primary requirements repository.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

mcq.q: Why should conflicting stakeholder requirements be surfaced during capture rather than hidden until design?
options: Because conflict helps reduce documentation effort
Because unresolved conflict can create false requirement certainty and expensive rework later
Because developers prefer ambiguity
Because all conflicts disappear in UAT
mcq.explanation: If conflicting requirements are left unresolved, the team may design against assumptions that later prove wrong, causing churn and rework.
wrongExplanations: Conflict does not reduce effort; it needs active management.
Developers do not benefit from ambiguity.
UAT is too late to resolve major requirement conflicts.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: Which statement best reflects good architect judgment when capturing requirements for future-state business processes?
options: Copy the current process exactly to avoid resistance
Guide stakeholders to define the desired future process while challenging unnecessary legacy behavior
Avoid process discussions and capture only system features
Leave future-state design entirely to developers
mcq.explanation: The architect should facilitate future-state thinking and challenge legacy assumptions instead of preserving inefficient behavior by default.
wrongExplanations: Exact replication often preserves inefficiency.
Feature lists without process thinking are weak architect practice.
Future-state process design requires business and architect involvement.

msRef.label: Choose the right processes for your solution - Dynamics 365

tf.q: True or False: Capturing requirements includes refining high-level requirements into clearer functional and non-functional statements.
tf.explanation: The PL-600 study guide explicitly includes refining high-level requirements and identifying both functional and non-functional requirements as part of capture requirements.

msRef.label: Study guide for Exam PL-600: Microsoft Power Platform Solution Architect

tf.q: True or False: Workshops should only be used to confirm technical designs after requirements are already fixed.
tf.explanation: Microsoft guidance describes workshops as collaborative sessions used to clarify and refine functional requirements and align stakeholders before detailed design is finalized.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

tf.q: True or False: A good user story should communicate business value, not just implementation detail.
tf.explanation: User stories are most useful when they explain who needs something, what they need, and why it matters.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

tf.q: True or False: Requirement documentation should be usable later for design, testing, and scope control.
tf.explanation: Captured requirements are not just notes. They should support later design, validation, and governance decisions.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

dragdrop.instruction: Match each requirement-capture activity to the main purpose.
zones: Align stakeholders and clarify functional needs
Express user need and business value clearly
Turn ambiguity into a requirement that can guide design
Expose uncertain conditions that may affect solution decisions

correctMapping.Facilitate a design workshop: Align stakeholders and clarify functional needs
correctMapping.Write a user story: Express user need and business value clearly
correctMapping.Refine a vague stakeholder statement: Turn ambiguity into a requirement that can guide design
correctMapping.Document assumptions: Expose uncertain conditions that may affect solution decisions

dragdrop.explanation: Good requirement capture is not one activity. It combines facilitated discussion, refinement, structured expression of needs, and explicit handling of uncertainty.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

dragdrop.instruction: Match each example to the correct type.
items: As a sales manager, I want duplicate leads flagged so that my team avoids duplicate follow-up
The solution must support case creation within agreed response-time targets
Approvals can only be submitted when the mandatory fields are completed
zones: Rule that supports acceptance or validation

correctMapping.Approvals can only be submitted when the: Rule that supports acceptance or validation

dragdrop.explanation: Architect-level requirement capture includes distinguishing strong requirement forms from vague statements that still need refinement.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

casestudy.title: Case Study - Capturing Requirements Without Locking in the Wrong Solution
casestudy.scenario: A customer wants to modernize its service request process. In workshops, one operations manager says the team needs a custom service console immediately. Frontline agents say the real problem is that requests arrive through too many channels, triage rules are inconsistent, and required information is often missing. Regional leads disagree on whether the same request flow can be used everywhere. The project team has notes from several meetings, but the statements are inconsistent, some are vague, and many are written as proposed solutions instead of requirements.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

questions.q: What should the solution architect do first?
options: Accept the custom console idea and estimate it
Facilitate structured refinement to separate business needs, process issues, and proposed solution ideas
Ignore the frontline input and rely on managers
Start UI wireframes for the console
questions.explanation: The first step is to structure and refine what has been heard so the team is solving the real problem, not just the loudest suggested solution.
wrongExplanations: That would lock the project onto an unvalidated approach.
Frontline input is critical for understanding process reality.
Wireframing is premature before the requirements are clarified.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

questions.q: Which captured statement is the best candidate for refinement rather than direct acceptance?
options: Requests arrive through too many channels
We need a custom service console immediately
Required information is often missing
Regional leads disagree on standardization
questions.explanation: This is a proposed solution statement, not a validated requirement. It needs to be unpacked into the actual business need and success outcome.
wrongExplanations: This is a real problem statement that can inform requirements.
This is a useful process/data pain point, not merely a premature design choice.
This is an alignment issue that must be managed, not just a solution suggestion.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

questions.q: What is the best way to document the requirement that agents need enough information to triage accurately?
options: Build more fields
As an agent, I want required request details captured consistently so that I can triage requests correctly on first review
Users need a better screen
Add a plugin
questions.explanation: This user story expresses actor, need, and business value clearly, making it more useful for design and validation.
wrongExplanations: This is an implementation guess, not a requirement expression.
This is too vague and not clearly tied to process outcome.
This is a technical solution idea.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

questions.q: Why is it important to document the disagreement between regional leads rather than smooth over it in the notes?
options: Because unresolved differences can affect scope, process design, and standardization decisions
Because conflict always improves innovation
Because developers prefer different regional rules
Because it avoids future workshops
questions.explanation: Regional disagreement is not noise. It is a real requirement and governance issue that can materially affect scope and future-state process design.
wrongExplanations: Conflict is not useful by itself unless managed productively.
Developer preference is not the main reason to capture the issue.
It does not remove the need for future workshops.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects
